citation: Usoh, M., Catena, E., Arman, S., & Slater, M. (2000). Using presence questionnaires in reality. Presence: Teleoperators and Virtual Environments, 9(5), 497-503.
original_reference: Slater, M., Usoh, M., & Steed, A. (1994). Depth of presence in virtual environments. Presence, 3(2), 130-144.
intended_use: Immersive virtual environments; designed for VR-specific spatial presence measurement

none.note: SUS is a unidimensional instrument — no subscales. All 6 items load on a single presence factor.

items.text: In the virtual environment I had a sense of 'being there'.
To what extent were there times during the experience when the virtual environment was the reality for you?
When you think back about your experience, do you think of the virtual environment more as images you saw, or more as somewhere you visited?
During the virtual experience, did you often think to yourself that you were actually in the virtual environment?
To what extent did you feel that the virtual environment surrounded you?
During the virtual experience, how often did you feel disoriented with respect to where you were in the real world?

scoring_instructions.method: Sum or mean of all 6 items
scoring_instructions.common_practice: Items SUS1-SUS3 are most frequently cited; some studies use only these 3

psychometric_properties.brevity_advantage: Shortest validated presence questionnaire; minimal respondent burden

key_differentiators: Shortest of the canonical presence scales (6 items)
Dominant in Presence journal community (Rosakranse & Oh, 2014)
Recommended by Skarbez et al. (2017) for direct presence measurement
Bouchard et al. single-item version further reduces to 1 item
theoretical_grounding: Slater's Place Illusion (PI) framework; suspension-of-disbelief model of presence
